humanities_role_explanation: EduArt utilizes human-authored educational questions from secondary school tests and AP exams (content) to evaluate specialized domain capabilities such as visual reasoning, stylistic reasoning, and iconographic interpretation (domain).
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Visual question answering dataset that pairs low-to-medium resolution artwork images with structured and open-ended text questions.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates a model's capacity for factual recall (e.g., identifying style or technique) and visual/interpretive reasoning (e.g., reading symbolic meaning from visual details), alongside complex text completion and finding historical/factual errors in written texts.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: It is a domain-specific evaluation focused specifically on Art History and visual reasoning, rather than a general-purpose benchmark.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates twelve generative multimodal models including GPT-5.5, GPT-5.4 Mini/Nano, Gemini 3.1 Pro/Flash, Gemini 3.5 Flash, Claude Opus/Sonnet 4.6, Claude Haiku 4.5, Qwen3-VL-235B, Mistral Large 3, and Pixtral Large.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The primary systems under evaluation are multimodal Large Language Models.

arts_humanities_data.note: Visual Arts and Performing Arts, History

quantitative_metrics.note: Accuracy, exact match, F1 score, and Classical Test Theory (CTT) item discrimination (point-biserial correlation).

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, and the benchmark evaluates bilingual datasets in English and Italian.

summary: EduArt is an educational-level benchmark containing 871 human-designed questions from AP Art History and MyZanichelli Italian textbook exercises to diagnose art-historical knowledge and visual reasoning in LLMs. The paper uses Classical Test Theory to assess item-level characteristics and analyzes how different formats and explanation requirements affect model accuracy.
key_findings: Top frontier models score near-ceiling (92-97%) on multiple-choice items, but performance drops substantially on more complex formats like open completion (Claude Opus falls to 23.9%) and error identification (Claude Sonnet falls to 6.2%). Furthermore, requiring models to justify their answers (motivation condition) improves Claude models' accuracy slightly but degrades performance for GPT and Gemini models.
